speaker: interviewee
text: By the way, I want to-- that fellow was with me the 18 days? His name is Israel Scharf. He was from Krakow, too, not far from my hometown. We both were liberated. He went back to Krakow. But I didn't go nowhere. I was still there because I wasn't well. I was still-- had the effect from the sickness. And I lost his-- I knew his name was Israel. I just completely forgot his last name. And he forgot my last name. We looked for each other all these years. And you know, name Israel. He remember Pincus. But he didn't know my last name. We didn't see each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, last year in Miami, there was a gathering. You know about it? The gathering for the survivors. And I put on a shirt, a T-shirt. And I printed my name and what camp I was. And this woman reporter happened to see me. So she interviewed me. And she-- I didn't even know if she's gonna put it in the paper. And she put it in the

speaker: interviewee
text: He lives in Miami during the summer-- during the winter, the same Israel. And he saw this name-- Pincus Kolender from Bochnia. And something struck him. He said, I think this is the fellow who was with me. [INAUDIBLE] And he called me up to the hotel. And we met there, after 50 years. I got pictures of that we take together. It was very interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now we're in contact. He lives in Miami. And I-- and he calls me up often. I call him up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of the farmer that helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We went there two years ago. I took my wife and another friend. We went to Czechoslovakia. I wanted to see it. I really wanted to. But it's 50 years. We got there. There's not a trace there. The house is gone. And I asked some people in the neighborhood. 50 years then. And mostly younger people-- they don't know anything. So I was very disappointed. I really wanted-- I thought, maybe I'll find those people. Well, I presume they're dead. But the house is gone. And so we spent a couple hours and came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the town or the area that you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Katowice. Katowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were there until May the 7th. May the 7th, the farmer came [? to let him, ?] and tell us that Americans are here. You are liberated. Because we went to town, reported to the-- to the, you know, headquarters, with Americans. And right away, I'd get treatment. We went there, the doctor. And checked me out. And fortunately, I was really-- I was healthy, just undernourished and malnutrition. And believe me, we wanted to work-- me and this other fella. So we volunteered to work for KP, for an American company. And they were glad to accept us and-- with it. And we surprised. In four weeks, I put on-- I was maybe 80 or 90 pounds when-- when I was liberated. And in a month to five weeks, I was back to normal. I have the picture here. You can see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard for you to trust the Americans after-- or going and register?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. As a matter of fact, I-- I didn't have any clothes. They gave me uniforms. I have to tell you. There's a little story there, too. And we had to uniform, American uniforms, of course without insignias. We were accepted. We-- we sleep-- slept with them in the barracks there. And it was lovely.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, work-- work didn't bother me because we were used to work. And the food was outstanding. The best, you know? It was excellent. And-- Which division was this? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a tank division. That's all I know. One tank division.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this fellow-- in the evenings, we had free time and borrowed a motorcycle. And we took it out for a ride, you know? And unfortunately, we had a accident. We turned over and-- right in the middle of the street on-- in-- in-- in this city. And this military police saw-- saw these two uniforms. They came to us. And we were [? so ?] down. We didn't have no identification, nothing. And he started talking to us in English. And we didn't understand a word. And so-- oh, they thought, oh, I know what they thought. We got some, here, some-- either we are Germans who try to, saboteurs, or something, you know? And they arrested us. Because [? what? ?] Guess where they arrested us? Together with Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a lot of Germans and POWs. And they arrested. They thought we are Germans. Boy, I-- I can't-- You cannot imagine the fear when there were Germans there. We just were to get separated. They came to us, start talking. And we didn't want to talk to them. We were afraid they might kill us. Finally, at night, the officer came. We told him we're Jewish. We told him. We even showed him the arms. [INAUDIBLE] We said we are prisoners from concentration camp. Evidently, they got-- they notified. And this Jewish soldier came-- office. And he spoke Yiddish. We told him. And they released us. [LAUGHS] That was an evening, I tell you. I can-- you cannot imagine. And they released us. And we were with this company for about a couple of months, six weeks. And then they moved. They went back to United States. The guys took a liking to us. And they said, you come with us. And maybe we can get you in the United States. And for sure enough, we went back with the whole tank division, all the way to Bremen Hafen. We went with them. And when we got to Bremen Hafen, when we start loading on the ship, we didn't have nothing to show. They told us they could sneak us in with-- with them. And we couldn't get in on the ship. And they brought us back, had to send us back to Czechoslovakia. We were there till about another few weeks. And then we decided to go to West Germany because all the concentrations of all the displaced persons were in West Germany. So we went to this Pocking. I don't know if you heard. It was a DP camp. We went to Pocking. And then from Pocking, we decided I didn't like to-- to live in a DP camp. So we went to a little town the name of Simbach. It is right on the Austrian border. And we lived there about five years, from 1945 till 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you make a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Germans gave us rations. And that help us. So food-- we had plenty of food. It was no problem. It was no problem. And we applied for the visa. And I applied to the United States in 1945. And it took us five years, five years to get the visa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard to trust German government again, to register with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't-- we lived there. It was very-- very friendly, very friendly to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe they were afraid. You know? They didn't want-- but very friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any anti-Semitism in that area after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: If there was, no. We didn't-- we didn't notice it. Not-- not from the Germans. Like I say, that was right after the war. They were still under occupation. So they didn't show any anti-Semitism. On contrary, they were very, very friendly. I made a lot of friends there, German friends. Until 1950-- finally the visa. I got my visa in January of 1950. And I came the United States January the 27th, arrived in New York. And my visa was for Charleston. Why they pick me to Charleston, I don't know. Just out of thousands, I was picked to Charleston. I told my wife she's lucky that I came here. [LAUGHS] And I came to Charleston in January. And in July of the same year-- I was barely six months in the United States-- the Korean War broke out. And I was drafted in the US army. And I was sent back to Germany for a tour of duty. [LAUGHS] A twist of fate.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you, to go back in Germany as someone--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to. I try, I begged-- I went to the company. You know, I was not sent right away. I was stationed in Fort Benning. And I was stationed in Fort Bragg. Then the company was shipped. It was-- had orders to go to Germany. And I didn't particularly like to go there. And I went to the company commander and asked him, maybe, can release me from this. And he said, orders are orders. So I was only six months there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where in Germany were you sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kaiserslautern. And I was also in France, in Metz, France, stationed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you stationed in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: About six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn English when you first came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I went to the army, I volunteered. I want to go. And so they had classes in the-- in the company, there. So I took three days. For three months, I took classes, English. I went, took lessons there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you first came to Charleston, did any group help you get settled?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the HIAS helped me. And the Jewish Community Center helped me, from the beginning. Well-- although I got a job. After a while, I got a job. And so I-- I didn't need any help.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out what happened to the rest of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was right after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I just knew. You sensed it. Well, I knew my mother's dead. I knew my sister is dead. The only one I didn't know is about my-- my brother. And I-- I looked for him all over. Wherever the DP camps, I sent my-- his name and checked and get no response. I knew he's dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- and-- I found out he was killed in Plaszow, right after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I found out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you received by the Jewish community in Charleston when you first--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very good. Very friendly. Nothing but good things to say about it. And I was accepted [? right ?] [? there. ?] And I'm now also accepted like a-- like a native.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone ask you about your experiences during the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you experience any anti-Semitism in Charleston?

speaker: interviewee
text: You hear some remarks, you know? But not personally. They didn't attack me, didn't-- no. Yeah. I even heard it in the Army, a few, some remarks, you know, you're the Jew, or something like that. But it's natural. You always hear that so much. But-- but that's about all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first memory of Charleston? How-- how did you get here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I came by train from-- from New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and you left where in Europe to come to New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Bremen Hafen. Bremen Hafen is a port.

speaker: interviewer
text: By ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I came by ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then from New York, by train to Charleston.

speaker: interviewee
text: By train to Charleston. Right. The irony, when I was in the army, I came with this troop transport. And the name was General [? McRay. ?] And I went-- this is the one that I came from Europe. I went to the army. They sent me back to Europe with the same ship, [LAUGHS] same transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through a-- you know, the event. I remember it was [? summer. ?] We went. And I went there. It was in the center. No. It was in the synagogue, social hall.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Charleston?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody introduced us, said she's a survivor. And so we start talking and gradually build a friendship. And it became we were married.

speaker: interviewer
text: You emigrated January of 1950.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And she was here already since three years. She came in 1947. So she had family here. And so they brought her here in 1947. I had nobody. So I had to wait for the visa. And I came in 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you got out of the army, where did you work? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I decided-- people told me, why don't you go and start-- I had no profession. And I had from experience, when I was here the first few months, without any profession, you work with somebody, they pay you nothing. So other people told me, why don't you get yourself a old car and start peddling? That was-- a lot of people did it in the '20s and the '30s. Although I didn't particularly like it, but I couldn't help it. You have to live. And I started peddling. I worked for about five years. I-- I sold, in the countryside, clothes. And then I decided. I made a lot of customers, made a lot of friends. And I opened up a little store, furniture and appliance store. And that was till five years. Made a nice living out of it. Then I sold it. And I retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- what year did you start your store?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the 1958.

speaker: interviewer
text: The name of the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Globe Furniture Company. G-L-O-B-E. And I retired in six-- 1991.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult for you, coming here by yourself with no family and not knowing the language?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely. Definitely. Definitely. It was very difficult because without you lonely. You can't speak the language. And you have no friends. It is terrible. You feel terrible, believe me. And that was the worst time for anybody to come to a strange country. You don't know anybody. But gradually, just make friends. And once you make friends and get the language a little bit, it's-- it's different. Then it got easier for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know about the Ku Klux Klan when you came to South Carolina? Had you heard anything about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. No. I didn't. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And had--

speaker: interviewee
text: I found out later, when I came here. But I didn't hear that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it concern you at all that there were anti-Jewish organizations in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just didn't hear about it. I was concerned when I came to this country. I remember from New York, they put us on train to Charleston. From New York to Washington, we are full a lot of black people. I was sitting all [INAUDIBLE]. By Washington, when we got to Washington, I noticed all the black people got out. I just happened to stand and look out. And they all went to the back. I just couldn't figure this out. You know, they had to it because this was the South. And that disturbed me a little bit, very much. I was disturbed about it. But that was the law then. You know, you couldn't-- couldn't help it. Also was the [INAUDIBLE] in the army. When I was in the army, they just start to integrate the black soldiers with white soldiers. And there was still plenty, plenty of racism. I remember, I-- I got acquainted with this soldier. It was a nice black guy. Him and me and another white fella. And we went-- that was in-- in Columbus, Georgia. We went to this restaurant. We'll have something to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: We went in. He came in with us. And they wouldn't let him. They wouldn't seat him. Said they couldn't serve him. And that was very disturbing to me because I know what it means. You have to go through that, you know? [INAUDIBLE] And that was in the ghetto, we [INAUDIBLE]. So you had to-- you had to take off your hat for a German. And you couldn't go. You had to be separated. You have to go through yourself to realize that. It's a very-- very-- very disturbing. Here's a nice soldier. He's fighting for our country. And he-- why can't he have the same? You can't have classes. How would you expect him to be a good soldier? Just like the Poles-- they expect us to be good citizens. How can you be a good citizen if the government discriminate against you? You know, in America-- in Poland, as far as you could make [INAUDIBLE] the captain. Anything above captain, a Jewish soldier couldn't make it. [INAUDIBLE] There were many Jewish soldiers, good soldiers. And they served the Polish army well, the Polish people. But they had classes. And so far, you could go to up to captain. Anything above, you couldn't make it. So how do you expect-- if you are discriminate against your citizens, how do you expect them to be a good citizen, right? That's the way it is. Did you play any part in the Civil Rights Movement in-- in South Carolina?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be honest, no. I was too busy making a living. I had a family to support. I was too busy. I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Speaking of your family, tell me a little bit about your children-- their ages and when they were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Marc was born in 1955. He went to [INAUDIBLE] here. It is a very fine school. And then he went to University of North Carolina. And he went, here, to medical university. He's now-- he's a pediatrician. And he's married. He's got two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the names of those grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Ari ?] and David. Now, my-- my other son, also, he went to Emory, in Georgia, which also a fine school. And he graduated tax law from South Carolina in Columbia, South Carolina Law School. And he also went to George Washington Law School. He-- he was specialized in tax law. And he's doing very well. He's working for a big firm in Washington. And he's got two children. And he's married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they--

speaker: interviewee
text: And his name-- the name is Daniel, the oldest boy. And the daughter is Elise.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Jeffrey was born in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- He was born in '58. '58. And I have a daughter, Risa. She graduated. She went here to a good school, too, [? College ?] [? Prep, ?] which is a private school. And she went to George Washington. She graduated in George Washington. She's a special education teacher. She works in Washington. She's a daycare center director. And she's still available. She's my only worry, now. I'd like to see her get married and get off my back. [LAUGHS] I still have to help her. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your experiences in Europe during the Holocaust affect the way that you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were a little too, what do you say, too careful with them, you know, especially my wife-- over-protective. It's most of the survivors have the same thing, probably, very over-protective. I think that was-- that's-- it was a little problem, that we're too over-protective. But they all turned out to be good kids and never had any problem. And they always give us only joy. And we had no problems with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you teach them about tolerance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely. My daughter, and my children-- they cannot hear. If they hear the word, any [? sometimes ?] remark about blacks, she gets angry. Even though I don't mean anything by that. She said, why do you say she's black? She's American. Why do you say she's black or something like that? She don't like to hear that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said that you've returned, several times, to Europe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Twice. Twice. I took my children in 19-- it was eight years ago. Must have been 1987 or '88. We went to Poland. I wanted to see my hometown. I took them to Auschwitz. And I wanted to see how life was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like, going back to those areas?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very emotional, the first few-- very emotional, especially I went to my hometown. I went-- my house is still there. And I went in there. And it was traumatic. I went to the cemetery. And I went to Plaszow. But I found it, the mass grave that my father's buried. And of course, I went to my hometown. And I looked for that-- for that metal box. And I couldn't find it. So it was traumatic and very emotional.

speaker: interviewer
text: How have your experiences during the Holocaust shaped your faith?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to be honest, for a while, the first-- from the beginning, I didn't. I lost faith. I lost faith. I didn't believe in anything anymore. But when I came to the United States, and you settle and build a family, all things came back to me. And now I'm-- I'm religious. I'm Orthodox. We believe in-- in dietary laws. We don't eat the ham or bacon, something like that. And I go, right now, go to services every Friday, Saturday, and Sunday. And I hope my children will continue, will be the same ways.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's been over 50 years since the Holocaust and the end--

speaker: interviewee
text: 51 years. 51.

speaker: interviewer
text: --of World War II. Do you think about your experiences on a daily basis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, especially now, for the last five years which I'm very active in lectures. And every time when you speak about your past and about the Holocaust, you get flashbacks at night. But it's something I accepted.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things, when you have flashbacks at night, do you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see the corpses, dead bodies. You think about your mother, when you saw her get killed. It's a lot of things. Not anything particular. But it's so many things you think back.

speaker: interviewer
text: If it's so difficult for you, why do you go to schools, and are you involved in other Holocaust activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I feel it's-- it's necessary. Because somebody got to do it. If we don't do it now, in another 20 years, once we are gone, it's gonna be on the history books. You've got already, now, revisionists who deny the Holocaust ever existed. And this is-- this is very painful to us. So therefore, I feel it's my obligation to inform and educate those kids and tell them that, actually, the Holocaust did happen. And maybe by education, we can prevent another Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: If someone were to watch this video tape years from now, what one thing would you want them to take away and remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Remember what happened to the six million. And think about it. And they should know that should never happen again, what happened to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything I haven't asked you about that you think is important for people to know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that's about all. No. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this is photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandfather, my father's father. His name was Moshe Kolender.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when?

speaker: interviewee
text: When this is taken, I have no idea. It must have been taken in the '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bochnia, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is, actually, a driver's license and identification card. It was taken in August of 1945. You can see in the picture, I was already recuperating. I was-- I had my hair already. I was in good shape then. My jacket is actually from Auschwitz. You see the striped jacket? I kept it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Simbach. I mean, that's in Bavaria. That's where the little town where I lived for five years after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cut. Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is myself. This is-- I was in the army. And that was taken in 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Fort Benning. I was in basic training.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is myself and my wife, Renee. We got married in November 1953. And this during the wedding. We cut the cake, then, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's Charleston, South Carolina.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Let's start with my oldest son in the top row with the glasses, Marc. In the middle, that's his wife, Ellen. And next to Ellen, that's my daughter Risa. And in the bottom row, there's Jeffrey and then his wife, Debbie, and their son, Daniel. I missed the other two boys. This is Marc's two boys. It's Ari on the top. And the bottom one is David. There is one more missing. Yeah.
